violations.description: The Order aggregate's CatalogItemOrdered value object embeds a denormalized snapshot of Catalog module data (name, price, image) inside the Order module's persistence model.

evidence.reason: Value object capturing catalog item details at order time, embedded in OrderItem

violations.migration_impact: If Catalog and Order become separate services, this snapshot pattern is actually beneficial (avoids runtime cross-service calls for historical order data) and should likely be preserved, not removed.
violations.recommendation: Confirm this is an intentional anti-corruption/snapshot pattern rather than accidental coupling; document it as a deliberate bounded-context boundary in forward engineering.

violations.description: Two separate UI surfaces appear to provide catalog item administration: src/Web/Pages/Admin/EditCatalogItem.cshtml (Razor Pages) and src/BlazorAdmin/Pages/CatalogItemPage/* (Blazor). This suggests overlapping ownership of the 'Admin' responsibility between the Web module/project and the BlazorAdmin app.

evidence.reason: Razor Pages admin catalog edit surface
Blazor admin catalog edit surface for the same entity

violations.migration_impact: Forward engineering an 'Admin' module/service would need to decide which UI is canonical; carrying both forward duplicates effort and risks divergent business rules.
violations.recommendation: Confirm with the team whether src/Web/Pages/Admin is legacy/deprecated in favor of BlazorAdmin, or whether both are actively maintained and serve different audiences.

violations.description: BlazorAdmin's catalog services (e.g. CachedCatalogItemServiceDecorator) appear to wrap calls into PublicApi's CatalogItemEndpoints, meaning the admin frontend's data contracts are coupled to PublicApi's endpoint request/response shapes.

evidence.reason: Decorator service for catalog item operations in the admin frontend
Corresponding backend endpoint for catalog item creation, likely consumed by the above decorator

violations.migration_impact: Any change to PublicApi catalog endpoint contracts (request/response DTOs, routes) requires coordinated changes in BlazorAdmin - a cross-deployable-unit coupling that complicates independent service extraction of Catalog.
violations.recommendation: Introduce a versioned/contract-tested API boundary (e.g. shared DTO library with explicit versioning) between PublicApi and BlazorAdmin before extracting Catalog as an independent service.

violations.description: The Buyer and PaymentMethod entities (BuyerAggregate) have no corresponding service, interface, or controller/endpoint found anywhere in the provided evidence packs, despite being referenced indirectly by Order specifications (CustomerOrdersSpecification).

evidence.reason: Entity exists but no IBuyerService/BuyerService or BuyerController/Endpoint found in any evidence pack
Order queries reference customer/buyer identifiers, implying Buyer is used but its owning component is not identified

violations.migration_impact: Without a clear owning component, it is unclear whether Buyer should become part of an Order/Customer service, an Identity service, or its own module during forward engineering.
violations.recommendation: Search for BuyerService/IBuyerService or direct repository usage of Buyer in ApplicationCore/Services or Infrastructure (not present in the evidence packs supplied to this stage) and re-run module boundary detection once found.

violations.description: File src/BlazorAdmin/Services/CachedCatalogLookupDataServiceDecorator .cs contains a trailing space in its filename (before the .cs extension), as literally listed in the evidence pack source file lists.

evidence.reason: Filename as listed verbatim in component-registry-pack and layering-pattern-pack source_files_used arrays, containing a trailing space character

violations.migration_impact: Low direct impact, but malformed filenames can cause build/tooling issues on case-sensitive or path-strict systems (e.g. Linux CI, containerized builds) and may indicate the file is a stale duplicate never properly wired up.
violations.recommendation: Verify on disk whether this file exists with a trailing space, whether it is referenced by any .csproj/using statement, and whether it is a duplicate of CachedCatalogLookupDataServiceDecorator (without the space).

violations.description: PublicApi (an API/presentation-layer project) has a direct project reference to Infrastructure (a data-access/infrastructure-layer project), in addition to ApplicationCore. In strict Clean Architecture, outer layers may compose Infrastructure for DI registration, but if PublicApi endpoints call Infrastructure types directly (bypassing ApplicationCore interfaces), this would be a layer violation.
violations.migration_impact: If this reference is only used for composition-root DI registration in Program.cs (common and acceptable), impact is minimal. If individual endpoints import Infrastructure types directly, it weakens the ApplicationCore abstraction boundary and complicates swapping persistence implementations.
violations.recommendation: Audit PublicApi endpoint files for direct `using` statements referencing Infrastructure namespaces outside of Program.cs composition root.

violations.description: GuardExtensions and JsonExtensions in ApplicationCore/Extensions are positioned as cross-cutting utilities used broadly across Domain entities, Specifications, and Services, per their inclusion in nearly every evidence pack's source_files_used list.

evidence.reason: Appears in source_files_used across module-boundary, component-registry, dependency, and layering evidence packs, indicating broad usage
Appears in source_files_used across multiple evidence packs

violations.migration_impact: If every module depends on these shared extension methods, extracting any module as an independent service requires either duplicating these utilities or extracting them into a separate shared package first.
violations.recommendation: Treat GuardExtensions/JsonExtensions as part of a minimal 'shared kernel' package that would need to be published/versioned independently during forward engineering.
